is a [musical instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") in the [brass family](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument"). As with all brass instruments, sound is produced when the player's lips vibrate inside a mouthpiece, causing the [air column inside the instrument to vibrate](/wiki/Standing_wave "Standing wave"). Nearly all trombones use a telescoping slide mechanism to alter the [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)") instead of the [valves](/wiki/Brass_instrument_valve "Brass instrument valve") used by other brass instruments. The [valve trombone](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone") is an exception, using three valves similar to those on a [trumpet](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"), and the [superbone](/wiki/Superbone "Superbone") has valves and a slide.

The **trombone** is a [musical instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") in the [brass family](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument"). As with all brass instruments, sound is produced when the player's lips vibrate inside a mouthpiece, causing the [air column inside the instrument to vibrate](/wiki/Standing_wave "Standing wave"). Nearly all trombones use a telescoping slide mechanism to alter the [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)") instead of the [valves](/wiki/Brass_instrument_valve "Brass instrument valve") used by other brass instruments. The [valve trombone](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone") is an exception, using three valves similar to those on a [trumpet](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"), and the [superbone](/wiki/Superbone "Superbone") has valves and a slide.

The word *trombone* derives from Italian *tromba* (trumpet) and *-one* (a suffix meaning 'large'), so the name means 'large trumpet'. The trombone has a predominantly [cylindrical](/wiki/Cylinder "Cylinder") bore like the [trumpet](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"), in contrast to the more conical brass instruments like the [cornet](/wiki/Cornet "Cornet"), the [flugelhorn](/wiki/Flugelhorn "Flugelhorn"), the [baritone](/wiki/Baritone_horn "Baritone horn"), and the [euphonium](/wiki/Euphonium "Euphonium"). The two most frequently encountered variants are the [tenor trombone](/wiki/Tenor_trombone "Tenor trombone") and [bass trombone](/wiki/Bass_trombone "Bass trombone"); when the word *trombone* is mentioned alone; it is mostly taken to mean the tenor model. These are treated as [non-transposing instruments](/wiki/Transposing_instrument "Transposing instrument"), reading at concert pitch in bass clef, with higher notes sometimes being notated in tenor clef. They are pitched in B♭, an octave below the B♭ trumpet and an octave above the B♭ contrabass [tuba](/wiki/Tuba "Tuba"). The once common E♭ [alto trombone](/wiki/Alto_trombone "Alto trombone") became less common as improvements in technique extended the upper range of the tenor, but it is regaining popularity for its lighter sonority. In British brass-band music the tenor trombone is treated as a B♭ [transposing instrument](/wiki/Transposing_instrument "Transposing instrument"), written in treble clef, and the alto trombone is written at concert pitch, usually in alto clef.

A person who plays the trombone is called a trombonist or trombone player.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

*Trombone* comes from the Italian word *tromba* (trumpet) plus the suffix *-one* (large), meaning 'large trumpet'.

During the Renaissance, the equivalent English term was *[sackbut](/wiki/Sackbut "Sackbut")*. The word first appears in court records in 1495 as *shakbusshe*. *Shakbusshe* is similar to *sacabuche*, attested in Spain as early as 1478. The French equivalent *saqueboute* appears in 1466.[[1]](#cite_note-Michault-1)

The German *Posaune* long predates the invention of the slide and could refer to a natural trumpet as late as the early fifteenth century.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuion201022-2)

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origin")]

See also: [Sackbut](/wiki/Sackbut "Sackbut")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/MIMEd_6111._Tenor_sackbut_in_A%2C_Monk_model.png/250px-MIMEd_6111._Tenor_sackbut_in_A%2C_Monk_model.png)](/wiki/File:MIMEd_6111._Tenor_sackbut_in_A,_Monk_model.png)

Renaissance era tenor sackbut (replica by Frank Tomes, London)

The sackbut appeared in the 15th century and was used extensively across Europe, declining in most places by the mid to late 17th century. It was used in outdoor events, in concert, and in liturgical settings. Its principal role was as the [contratenor](/wiki/Countertenor "Countertenor") part in a dance band.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerbert200659-3) It was also used, along with [shawms](/wiki/Shawm "Shawm"), in bands sponsored by towns and courts. Trumpeters and trombonists were employed in German city-states to stand watch [in the city towers](/wiki/Tower_music "Tower music") and herald the arrival of important people to the city, an activity that signified wealth and strength in 16th-century German cities. These heralding trombonists were often viewed separately from the more skilled trombonists who played in groups such as the [alta capella](/wiki/Alta_capella "Alta capella") wind ensembles and the first orchestral ensembles, which performed in religious settings such as [St Mark's Basilica](/wiki/St_Mark%27s_Basilica "St Mark's Basilica") in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") in the early 17th century.[[4]](#cite_note-Green-4) The 17th-century trombone had slightly smaller dimensions than a modern trombone, with a bell that was more conical and less flared. Modern [period performers](/wiki/Historically_informed_performance "Historically informed performance") use the term "sackbut" to distinguish this earlier version of the trombone from the modern instrument.

Composers who wrote for trombone during this period include [Claudio Monteverdi](/wiki/Claudio_Monteverdi "Claudio Monteverdi"), [Heinrich Schütz](/wiki/Heinrich_Sch%C3%BCtz "Heinrich Schütz"), [Giovanni Gabrieli](/wiki/Giovanni_Gabrieli "Giovanni Gabrieli") and his uncle [Andrea Gabrieli](/wiki/Andrea_Gabrieli "Andrea Gabrieli"). The trombone doubled voice parts in sacred works, but there are also solo pieces written for trombone in the early 17th century.

When the sackbut returned to common use in England in the 18th century, Italian music was so influential that the instrument became known by its Italian name, "trombone".[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuion1988p._3:_"Many_modern_musicians_prefer_to_use_the_word_'sackbut'_when_referring_to_the_Baroque_trombone._All_other_instruments_in_constant_use_since_the_Baroque_have_changed_more...In_response_to_the_number_of_times_people_including_musicians,_have_asked_if_the_sackbut_is_something_like_a_trombone,_I_have_stopped_using_this_misleading_word."-5) Its name remained constant in Italy ([*trombone*](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/trombone "it:trombone")) and in Germany ([*Posaune*](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Posaune "de:Posaune")).

During the later Baroque period, [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach") and [George Frideric Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel") used trombones on a few occasions. Bach called for a *tromba di tirarsi*, which may have been a form of the closely related [slide trumpet](/wiki/Slide_trumpet "Slide trumpet"), to double the [cantus firmus](/wiki/Cantus_firmus "Cantus firmus") in some liturgical [cantatas](/wiki/Cantata "Cantata").[[6]](#cite_note-6) He also employed a choir of four trombones to double the chorus in three of his cantatas ([BWV 2](/wiki/Ach_Gott,_vom_Himmel_sieh_darein,_BWV_2 "Ach Gott, vom Himmel sieh darein, BWV 2"), [BWV 21](/wiki/Ich_hatte_viel_Bek%C3%BCmmernis,_BWV_21 "Ich hatte viel Bekümmernis, BWV 21") and [BWV 38](/wiki/Aus_tiefer_Not_schrei_ich_zu_dir,_BWV_38 "Aus tiefer Not schrei ich zu dir, BWV 38")).[[7]](#cite_note-7) Because of the relative scarcity of trombones, their solo parts were generally interchangeable with other instruments.

### Classical period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Classical period")]

The construction of the trombone did not change very much between the Baroque and Classical period, but the bell became slightly more flared. [Christoph Willibald Gluck](/wiki/Christoph_Willibald_Gluck "Christoph Willibald Gluck") was the first major composer to use the trombone in an opera overture, in the opera *[Alceste](/wiki/Alceste_(Gluck) "Alceste (Gluck)")* (1767). He also used it in the operas *[Orfeo ed Euridice](/wiki/Orfeo_ed_Euridice "Orfeo ed Euridice")*, *[Iphigénie en Tauride](/wiki/Iphig%C3%A9nie_en_Tauride "Iphigénie en Tauride")* (1779), and *[Echo et Narcisse](/wiki/Echo_et_Narcisse "Echo et Narcisse")*.

Early Classical composers occasionally included concertante movements with alto trombone as a solo instrument in [divertimenti](/wiki/Divertimento "Divertimento") and [serenades](/wiki/Serenade "Serenade"); these movements are often extracted from the multi-movement works and performed as standalone alto trombone concerti. Examples include the Serenade in E♭ (1755) by [Leopold Mozart](/wiki/Leopold_Mozart "Leopold Mozart")[[8]](#cite_note-8) and Divertimento in D major (1764)[[9]](#cite_note-9) by [Michael Haydn](/wiki/Michael_Haydn "Michael Haydn"). The earliest known independent trombone concerto is probably the Concerto for Alto Trombone and Strings in B♭ (1769)[[10]](#cite_note-10) by [Johann Georg Albrechtsberger](/wiki/Johann_Georg_Albrechtsberger "Johann Georg Albrechtsberger").

[Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart") used the trombone in operas (notably in scenes featuring the Commendatore in *[Don Giovanni](/wiki/Don_Giovanni "Don Giovanni")*) and in sacred music. The prominent solo part in the *Tuba Mirum* section of his [Requiem](/wiki/Requiem_(Mozart) "Requiem (Mozart)") became a staple audition piece for the instrument. Aside from solo parts, Mozart's orchestration usually features a trio of alto, tenor and bass trombones, doubling the respective voices in the choir. The earliest known symphony featuring a trombone section is Symphony in C minor by [Anton Zimmermann](/wiki/Anton_Zimmermann "Anton Zimmermann").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The date is uncertain but it is most probably from the peak of the composer's activity in the 1770s. The earliest confident date for introducing the trombone to the symphony is therefore Zimmermann's death in 1781.

#### Transition to Romantic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Transition to Romantic period")]

Symphony in E♭ (1807) by Swedish composer [Joachim Nicolas Eggert](/wiki/Joachim_Nicolas_Eggert "Joachim Nicolas Eggert")[[12]](#cite_note-12) features an independent trombone part. [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven") is sometimes mistakenly credited with the trombone's introduction into the orchestra, having used it shortly afterwards in his [Symphony No. 5 in C minor](/wiki/Symphony_No._5_(Beethoven) "Symphony No. 5 (Beethoven)") (1808), [Symphony No. 6 in F major ("Pastoral")](/wiki/Symphony_No._6_(Beethoven) "Symphony No. 6 (Beethoven)"), and [Symphony No. 9 ("Choral")](/wiki/Symphony_No._9_(Beethoven) "Symphony No. 9 (Beethoven)").

### Romantic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Romantic period")]

#### 19th-century orchestras

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 19th-century orchestras")]

Trombones were included in operas, symphonies, and other compositions by [Felix Mendelssohn](/wiki/Felix_Mendelssohn_Bartholdy "Felix Mendelssohn Bartholdy"), [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven"), [Hector Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz"), [Franz Berwald](/wiki/Franz_Berwald "Franz Berwald"), [Charles Gounod](/wiki/Charles_Gounod "Charles Gounod"), [Franz Liszt](/wiki/Franz_Liszt "Franz Liszt"), [Gioacchino Rossini](/wiki/Gioacchino_Rossini "Gioacchino Rossini"), [Franz Schubert](/wiki/Franz_Schubert "Franz Schubert"), [Robert Schumann](/wiki/Robert_Schumann "Robert Schumann"), [Giuseppe Verdi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Verdi "Giuseppe Verdi"), and [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner"), and others.

The trombone trio was combined with one or two [cornetts](/wiki/Cornett "Cornett") during the Renaissance and early Baroque periods. The replacement of cornetts with oboes and clarinets did not change the trombone's role as a support to the alto, tenor, and bass voices of the chorus (usually in ecclesiastical settings), whose moving harmonic lines were more difficult to pick out than the melodic soprano line. The introduction of trombones into the orchestra allied them more closely with trumpets, and soon a tenor trombone replaced the alto. The Germans and Austrians kept alto trombone somewhat longer than the French, who preferred a section of three tenor trombones until after the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). In other countries, the trio of two tenor trombones and one bass became standard by about the mid-19th century.

Trombonists were employed less by court orchestras and cathedrals, who had been providing the instruments. Military musicians were provided with instruments, and instruments like the long F or E♭ bass trombone remained in military use until around the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). Orchestral musicians adopted the tenor trombone, as it could generally play any of the three trombone parts in orchestral scores.[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*]

Valve trombones in the mid-19th century did little to alter the make-up of the orchestral trombone section. While its use declined in German and French orchestras, the valve trombone remained popular in some countries, including Italy and [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), almost to the exclusion of the slide instrument. Composers such as Giuseppe Verdi, [Giacomo Puccini](/wiki/Giacomo_Puccini "Giacomo Puccini"), [Bedřich Smetana](/wiki/Bed%C5%99ich_Smetana "Bedřich Smetana"), and [Antonín Dvořák](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k "Antonín Dvořák") scored for a valve trombone section.

As the [ophicleide](/wiki/Ophicleide "Ophicleide") or the tuba was added to the orchestra during the 19th century, bass trombone parts were scored in a higher register than previously.[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] The bass trombone regained some independence in the early 20th century. Experiments with the trombone section included Richard Wagner's addition of a [contrabass trombone](/wiki/Contrabass_trombone "Contrabass trombone") in *[Der Ring des Nibelungen](/wiki/Der_Ring_des_Nibelungen "Der Ring des Nibelungen")* and Gustav Mahler's and Richard Strauss' addition of a second bass trombone to the usual trio of two tenors and one bass. The majority of orchestral works are still scored for the usual mid- to late-19th-century low brass section of two tenor trombones, one bass trombone, and one tuba.

#### 19th-century wind bands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 19th-century wind bands")]

Wind bands began during the French Revolution of 1791 and have always included trombones. They became more established in the 19th century and included circus bands, military bands, brass bands (primarily in the UK), and town bands (primarily in the US). Some of these, especially military bands in Europe, used rear-facing trombones with the bell pointing behind the player's left shoulder. These bands played a limited repertoire that consisted mainly of orchestral transcriptions, arrangements of popular and patriotic tunes, and feature pieces for soloists (usually cornetists, singers, and violinists). A notable work for wind band is Berlioz's 1840 *[Grande symphonie funèbre et triomphale](/wiki/Grande_symphonie_fun%C3%A8bre_et_triomphale "Grande symphonie funèbre et triomphale")*, which uses a trombone solo for the entire second movement. Toward the end of the 19th century, trombone virtuosi began appearing as soloists in American wind bands. [Arthur Pryor](/wiki/Arthur_Pryor "Arthur Pryor"), who played with the [John Philip Sousa](/wiki/John_Philip_Sousa "John Philip Sousa") band and formed his own band, was one of the most famous of these trombonists.

#### 19th-century pedagogy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 19th-century pedagogy")]

In the Romantic era, [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig") became a center of trombone pedagogy, and the instrument was taught at the [Musikhochschule](/wiki/University_of_Music_and_Theatre_Leipzig "University of Music and Theatre Leipzig") founded by [Felix Mendelssohn Bartholdy](/wiki/Felix_Mendelssohn_Bartholdy "Felix Mendelssohn Bartholdy"). The [Paris Conservatory](/wiki/Paris_Conservatory "Paris Conservatory") and its yearly exhibition also contributed to trombone education. At the Leipzig academy, Mendelssohn's bass trombonist, [Karl Traugott Queisser](/wiki/Karl_Traugott_Queisser "Karl Traugott Queisser"), was the first in a long line of distinguished professors of the trombone. Several composers wrote works for Queisser, including Mendelssohn's concertmaster [Ferdinand David](/wiki/Ferdinand_David_(musician) "Ferdinand David (musician)"), Ernst Sachse, and [Friedrich August Belcke](/wiki/Friedrich_August_Belcke "Friedrich August Belcke"). David wrote his *[Concertino for Trombone and Orchestra](/wiki/Trombone_Concertino_(David) "Trombone Concertino (David)")* in 1837, and Sachse's solo works remain popular in Germany. Queisser championed and popularized [Christian Friedrich Sattler](/wiki/Christian_Friedrich_Sattler "Christian Friedrich Sattler")'s tenor-bass trombone during the 1840s, leading to its widespread use in orchestras throughout Germany and Austria.

#### 19th-century construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 19th-century construction")]

Sattler had a great influence on trombone design, introducing a significantly larger bore (the most important innovation since the Renaissance), *Schlangenverzierungen* (snake decorations), the bell garland, and the wide bell flare. These features were widely copied during the 19th century and are still found on German made trombones.

The trombone was improved in the 19th century with the addition of "stockings" at the end of the inner slide to reduce friction, the development of the water key to expel condensation from the horn, and the occasional addition of [a valve](/wiki/F_attachment_tenor_trombone "F attachment tenor trombone") that was designed to be set in a single position but later became the modern F-valve. The valve trombone appeared around the 1850s shortly after the invention of valves, and was in common use in Italy and Austria in the second half of the century.

### Twentieth century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Twentieth century")]

Newsboy Military Band Member with Trombone, Toledo, Ohio

With the rise of recorded music and music schools, orchestral trombone sections around the world began to have a more consistent idea of a standard trombone sound. In the 1940s, British orchestras abandoned the use of small bore tenors and G basses in favor of the American/German choice of large bore tenors and B♭ basses. French orchestras did the same in the 1960s.

#### Use in jazz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Use in jazz")]

Further information: [List of jazz trombonists](/wiki/List_of_jazz_trombonists "List of jazz trombonists")

In the 1900s the trombone and the tuba played bass lines and outlined chords to support improvisation by the higher-pitched instruments. It began to be used as a solo instrument during the swing era of the mid-1920s. [Jack Teagarden](/wiki/Jack_Teagarden "Jack Teagarden") and [J. J. Johnson](/wiki/J._J._Johnson "J. J. Johnson") were early trombone soloists.[[13]](#cite_note-Bernotas-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Wilken-14)

#### 20th-century construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 20th-century construction")]

The trombone's construction changed in the 20th century. Different materials were used, mouthpiece, bore, and bell dimensions increased, and different mutes and valves were developed. Despite the overall trend towards larger bore instruments, many European trombone makers prefer a slightly smaller bore than their American counterparts.

One of the most significant changes was the development of the F-attachment trigger. Through the mid-20th century there was no need for orchestral trombonists to use instruments with the F attachment trigger. As contemporary composers such as Mahler began to write lower passages for the trombone, the trigger became necessary.

### Contemporary use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Contemporary use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Gallowstreet_at_Trans_Musicales_2024%2C_01.jpg/250px-Gallowstreet_at_Trans_Musicales_2024%2C_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gallowstreet_at_Trans_Musicales_2024,_01.jpg)

[Gallowstreet](/wiki/Gallowstreet "Gallowstreet")'s trombonist at [Trans Musicales 2024](/wiki/Rencontres_Trans_Musicales "Rencontres Trans Musicales"), Rennes, France

The trombone can be found in symphony orchestras, concert bands, [big bands](/wiki/Big_bands "Big bands"), marching bands, military bands, brass bands, and brass choirs. In chamber music, it is used in [brass quintets](/wiki/Brass_quintet "Brass quintet"), quartets, and trios, and also in trombone groups ranging from trios to choirs. A trombone choir can vary in size from five to twenty or more members. Trombones are also common in swing, jazz, merengue, salsa, R&B, [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), and New Orleans brass bands.

In contemporary [experimental music](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music"), [John Cage](/wiki/John_Cage "John Cage")'s collaboration with [Frank Rehak](/wiki/Frank_Rehak "Frank Rehak"), *Solo for sliding trombone* (1957/58), and [Luciano Berio](/wiki/Luciano_Berio "Luciano Berio")'s [*Sequenza V*](/wiki/Sequenza_V "Sequenza V") (1966) for solo trombone, influenced composers and performers such as [Stuart Dempster](/wiki/Stuart_Dempster "Stuart Dempster"), [Vinko Globokar](/wiki/Vinko_Globokar "Vinko Globokar"), and [Mike Svoboda](/wiki/Mike_Svoboda "Mike Svoboda"). Solo and chamber music compositions in this style may utilize [extended techniques](/wiki/Extended_technique "Extended technique"), including vocalized, [multiphonic](/wiki/Multiphonic "Multiphonic"), and percussive sounds. The trombone's slide enables the use of [microtonality](/wiki/Microtonality "Microtonality"), and modifying the F-attachment can be used to control a second bell or other instruments.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvobodaRoth201765–68-15)

## Construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Construction")]

Basic trombone anatomy

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | 1. tuning slide 2. counterweight 3. mouthpiece 4. slide lock ring 5. bell 6. knob/bumper 7. water key/spit valve 8. main slide 9. second slide brace/stay 10. first slide brace/stay 11. bell lock nut |

The trombone is a predominantly cylindrical tube with two U-shaped bends and a flared bell at the end. The tubing is approximately cylindrical but contains a complex series of tapers which affect the instrument's intonation. As with other [brass instruments](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument"), sound is produced by blowing air through pursed lips producing a vibration that creates a [standing wave](/wiki/Standing_wave "Standing wave") in the instrument.

The detachable cup-shaped [mouthpiece](/wiki/Mouthpiece_(brass) "Mouthpiece (brass)") is similar to that of the [baritone horn](/wiki/Baritone_horn "Baritone horn") and closely related to that of the [trumpet](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"). It has a [venturi](/wiki/Venturi_effect "Venturi effect"):[[16]](#cite_note-Friedman-2003-16) a small constriction of the air column that adds resistance, greatly affecting the tone of the instrument. The slide section consists of a [leadpipe](/wiki/Leadpipe "Leadpipe"), inner and outer slide tubes, and bracing, or "stays". The soldered stays on modern instruments replaced the loose stays found on [sackbuts](/wiki/Sackbut "Sackbut") (medieval precursors to trombones).[[17]](#cite_note-Campbell2004-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Fischer1984-18)

The most distinctive feature of the trombone is the slide that lengthens the tubing and lowers the pitch (cf. [valve trombone](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone")). During the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), sleeves (called "stockings") were developed to decrease friction that would impede the slide's motion. These were soldered onto the ends of the inner slide tubes to slightly increase their diameter. The ends of inner slides on modern instruments are manufactured with a slightly larger diameter to achieve the same end. This part of the slide must be lubricated frequently. The slide section is connected to the bell section by the neckpipe and a U-bend called the bell or back bow. The joint connecting the slide and bell sections has a threaded collar to secure the connection. Prior to the early 20th century this connection was made with friction joints alone.

Trombones have a short tuning slide in the U-shaped bend between the neckpipe and the bell, a feature designed by the French maker François Riedlocker in the early 19th century. It was incorporated into French and British designs, and later to German and American models, although German trombones were built without tuning slides well into the 20th century. Many types of trombone also include one or more [rotary valves](/wiki/Rotary_valves "Rotary valves") connected to additional tubing which lengthens the instrument. This extends the low range of the instrument and creates the option of using alternate slide positions for many notes.

Like the trumpet, the trombone is considered a cylindrical bore instrument since it has extensive sections of tubing that are of unchanging diameter (the slide section must be cylindrical in order to function). Tenor trombones typically have a bore of 0.450 inches (11.4 mm) (small bore) to 0.547 inches (13.9 mm) (large or *orchestral* bore) after the leadpipe and through the slide. The bore expands through the bow to the bell, which is typically between 7 and 8+1⁄2 inches (18 and 22 cm). A number of common variations on trombone construction are noted below.

### Bells

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Bells")]

Trombone bells (and sometimes slides) may be constructed of different [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass") mixtures. The most common material is yellow brass (70% copper, 30% zinc), but other materials include rose brass (85% copper, 15% zinc) and red brass (90% copper, 10% zinc). Some manufacturers offer interchangeable bells. Tenor trombone bells are usually between 7 and 9 in (18–23 cm) in diameter, with most being between 7+1⁄2 and 8+1⁄2 in (19–22 cm). The smallest sizes are found on jazz trombones and older narrow-bore instruments, while the larger sizes are common on orchestral models. Bass trombone bells can be 10+1⁄2 in (27 cm) or more, with most being between 9+1⁄2 and 10 in (24 and 25 cm). The bell may be made from two separate brass sheets or from one single piece of metal, hammered on a mandrel to shape it. The edge of the bell may be finished with or without a piece of bell wire to secure it, which also affects the tone quality; most bells are built with bell wire. Occasionally, trombone bells are made from solid [sterling silver](/wiki/Sterling_silver "Sterling silver").

### Valve attachments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Valve attachments")]

For trombones with three or more valves instead of a slide, see [valve trombone](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Trombone_F_slide_position_pedal_tones.png/250px-Trombone_F_slide_position_pedal_tones.png)](/wiki/File:Trombone_F_slide_position_pedal_tones.png)

Trombone with F attachment slide position second harmonics.[[19]](#cite_note-Orchestration-19)

Modern trombones often have a valve attachment, an extra loop of tubing attached to the bell section and engaged by a [valve](/wiki/Brass_instrument_valve "Brass instrument valve") operated by the left thumb by means of a [lever or trigger](/wiki/Brass_instrument#Trigger_or_throw "Brass instrument"). The valve attachment aids in increasing the lower range of the instrument, while also allowing alternate slide positions for difficult music passages. A valve can also make [trills](/wiki/Trill_(music) "Trill (music)") easier.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/TriggerTrombone.jpg/330px-TriggerTrombone.jpg)](/wiki/File:TriggerTrombone.jpg)

Tenor trombone with a traditional wrap F attachment

The valve attachment was originally developed by German instrument maker [Christian Friedrich Sattler](/wiki/Christian_Friedrich_Sattler "Christian Friedrich Sattler") in the late 1830s for the *Tenorbaßposaune* (lit. 'tenor-bass trombone'), a B♭ tenor trombone built with the wider bore and larger bell of a bass trombone that Sattler had earlier invented in 1821. Sattler's valve attachment added about 3 feet (0.9 m) of tubing to lower the fundamental pitch from B♭ to F, controlled by a rotary valve, and is essentially unchanged in modern instruments.

Valve attachments are most commonly found on tenor and bass trombones, but they can appear on sizes from soprano to contrabass.

Soprano
:   In the early 2010s Torbjörn Hultmark of the [Royal College of Music](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Music "Royal College of Music") commissioned the first soprano trombone in B♭ with an F valve, built by Thein Brass.[[20]](#cite_note-janesalmon-blog-2016-20)

Alto
:   Although rare on the E♭ alto trombone, a valve attachment usually lowers the instrument a perfect fourth into B♭, providing the first five or six positions from the tenor trombone slide. Some alto models have what is called a *trill valve*, providing a small loop of tubing that lowers the instrument by only a minor or major second, into D or D♭ respectively.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYeo202110"alto_trombone"-21)

Tenor
:   Tenor trombones, especially the larger bore symphonic models, commonly have a valve attachment which lowers the instrument from B♭ to F.

:   It provides access to the otherwise missing notes between the [pedal](/wiki/Pedal_tone "Pedal tone") B♭1 in first position, and the second partial E2 in seventh, as well as providing alternate slide positions for other notes in long (sixth and seventh) positions. Because the attachment tubing increases the length of the overall instrument by one-third, the distances between slide positions must also be one-third longer when the valve is engaged, resulting in only six positions available on the F slide, to low C2. Thus, the F attachment cannot provide the low B♮1, but it usually has a sufficiently long tuning slide to lower it into E as required, which will provide B♮1 in a very long position.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYeo202155"F-attachment"-22)

:   Tenor trombones without a valve are sometimes known as *straight* trombones.

Bass
:   Further information: [Bass trombone § Construction](/wiki/Bass_trombone#Construction "Bass trombone")
:   The modern bass trombone usually has two valve attachments to provide all of the notes that are absent on an instrument with no valves (B♮1 – E2). This allows the player to produce a complete [chromatic range](/wiki/Chromatic_scale "Chromatic scale") upwards from the pedal register.

:   The first valve is an F attachment the same as that found on a tenor trombone and extends the range down to C2. The second valve, engaged together with the first, lowers the instrument to D (or less commonly, E♭) and provides the low B1. The second valve can be *dependent*, where it serves to lower the F attachment to D and has no effect alone. More commonly the second valve is *independent*, where it can be engaged separately to lower the instrument to G♭, or to D when both are engaged.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYeo202173"independent_valves"-23)

:   Single-valve B♭ bass trombones with an F attachment are still made but are now less common than two-valve bass trombones. They are essentially very large bore tenor trombones, and likewise cannot provide the low B♮1 without lowering the valve to E with a long tuning slide.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuion201061-24)

Contrabass
:   Further information: [Contrabass trombone § Construction](/wiki/Contrabass_trombone#Construction "Contrabass trombone")
:   Contrabass trombones in F typically have two independent valves, tuned either to C and D♭ combining to A, or in European models tuned to D and B♭ combining to A♭. Contrabass trombones in low B♭ usually have only one valve in F, although Miraphone make a model in C with two independent valves in G and A♭, which combine to E.[[25]](#cite_note-contra-manufacturers-Bb-25)

#### Valve types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Valve types")]

See also: [Axial flow valve](/wiki/Axial_flow_valve "Axial flow valve") and [Hagmann valve](/wiki/Hagmann_valve "Hagmann valve")

[![Trombone with rotary valve](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Rotary_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg/250px-Rotary_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rotary_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg)

[![Trombone with axial flow valve](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Axial_flow_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg/250px-Axial_flow_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Axial_flow_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg)

[![Trombone with Hagmann valve](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Hagmann_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg/250px-Hagmann_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hagmann_valve_on_tenor_trombone.jpg)

Trombone valve attachments. Standard rotary valve, *left*; Thayer [axial flow valve](/wiki/Axial_flow_valve "Axial flow valve"), *center*; [Hagmann valve](/wiki/Hagmann_valve "Hagmann valve"), *right*.

The most common type of valve seen for valve attachments is the [rotary valve](/wiki/Rotary_valve "Rotary valve"), appearing on most band instruments, as well as most student and intermediate model trombones. Many improvements of the rotary valve, as well as entirely new and radically different valve designs, have been invented since the mid 20th century to give the trombone a more open, free sound than the tight bends in conventional rotary valve designs would allow. Many of these new valve designs have been widely adopted by players, especially in symphony orchestras. The Thayer [axial flow valve](/wiki/Axial_flow_valve "Axial flow valve") is offered on professional models from most trombone manufacturers, and the [Hagmann valve](/wiki/Hagmann_valve "Hagmann valve") particularly from European manufacturers.

Some trombones have three [piston](/wiki/Piston_valve "Piston valve") or [rotary](/wiki/Rotary_valve "Rotary valve") valves instead of a slide; see [valve trombone](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone").

#### Tubing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Tubing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Yamaha_Trombone_comparison_of_open_and_traditional_wrap.jpg/250px-Yamaha_Trombone_comparison_of_open_and_traditional_wrap.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yamaha_Trombone_comparison_of_open_and_traditional_wrap.jpg)

F attachment tubing: open wrap, *left*; traditional wrap, *right*.

[F attachment](/wiki/F_attachment "F attachment") tubing usually has a larger bore through the attachment than through the rest of the instrument. A typical slide bore for an orchestral tenor trombone is 0.547 in (13.9 mm) while the bore in the attachment is 0.562 in (14.3 mm). The attachment tubing also incorporates a tuning slide to tune the valve separately from the rest of the instrument, usually long enough to lower the pitch by a semitone when fully extended (from F to E on tenor and bass trombones, to reach the missing low B1).

Valve attachment tubing is often coiled tightly to keep within the bell section (*closed wrap*  or *traditional wrap*). A less coiled configuration, called *open wrap*, is found on some 19th and early 20th century instruments.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In the early 1980s, American instrument manufacturers began producing open wrap instruments after Californian instrument technician Larry Minick introduced open wraps around the same time that the [Thayer valve](/wiki/Axial_flow_valve "Axial flow valve") began to emerge among orchestral players.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Open wrap F attachment tubing is shaped in a single loop free of tight bends, resulting in a freer response and more "open" sound through the valve.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYeo202134"closed_wrap"-28)

In marching bands and other situations where the trombone may be more prone to damage, the confined traditional wrap is more common, since open wrap tubing protrudes behind the bell section.

### Tuning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Tuning")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Conn_70H_bass_trombone_in-slide_tuning.jpg/250px-Conn_70H_bass_trombone_in-slide_tuning.jpg)](/wiki/File:Conn_70H_bass_trombone_in-slide_tuning.jpg)

In-slide tuning on a Conn 70H bass trombone

Some trombones are tuned using a mechanism in the slide section instead of a tuning slide in the bell section. Having the tuning slide in the bell section (the more typical setup) requires two sections of cylindrical tubing in an otherwise conical part of the instrument, which affects the tone quality. Placing the tuning mechanism in the cylindrical slide section allows the bell section to remain conical.

### Slides

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Slides")]

Common and popular bore sizes for trombone slides are 0.500, 0.508, 0.525 and 0.547 in (12.7, 12.9, 13.3 and 13.9 mm) for tenor trombones, and 0.562 in (14.3 mm) for bass trombones. The slide may also be built with a *dual-bore* configuration, in which the bore of the second leg of the slide is slightly larger than the bore of the first leg, producing a stepwise conical effect. The most common dual-bore combinations are 0.481–0.491 in (12.2–12.5 mm), 0.500–0.508 in (12.7–12.9 mm), 0.508–0.525 in (12.9–13.3 mm), 0.525–0.547 in (13.3–13.9 mm), 0.547–0.562 in (13.9–14.3 mm) for tenor trombones, and 0.562–0.578 in (14.3–14.7 mm) for bass trombones.

### Mouthpiece

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Mouthpiece")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Schilke_51C4_Trombone_Mouthpiece.jpg/250px-Schilke_51C4_Trombone_Mouthpiece.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schilke_51C4_Trombone_Mouthpiece.jpg)

A tenor trombone mouthpiece

The mouthpiece is a separate part of the trombone and can be interchanged between similarly sized trombones from different manufacturers. Available mouthpieces for trombone (as with all brass instruments) vary in material composition, length, diameter, rim shape, cup depth, throat entrance, venturi aperture, venturi profile, outside design and other factors. Variations in mouthpiece construction affect the individual player's ability to make a lip seal and produce a reliable tone, the [timbre](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre") of that tone, its [volume](/wiki/Loudness "Loudness"), the instrument's [intonation](/wiki/Intonation_(music) "Intonation (music)") tendencies, the player's subjective level of comfort, and the instrument's playability in a given pitch range.

Mouthpiece selection is a highly personal decision. Thus, a symphonic trombonist might prefer a mouthpiece with a deeper cup and sharper inner rim shape in order to produce a rich symphonic tone quality, while a [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") trombonist might choose a shallower cup for brighter tone and easier production of higher notes. Further, for certain compositions, these choices between two such performers could easily be reversed. Some mouthpiece makers now offer mouthpieces that feature removable rims, cups, and shanks allowing players to further customize and adjust their mouthpieces to their preference.

### Plastic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Plastic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/WANZ_Instrument_2024-09_IMG_7761_pBone_plastic_trombone_%28crop%29.jpg/250px-WANZ_Instrument_2024-09_IMG_7761_pBone_plastic_trombone_%28crop%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:WANZ_Instrument_2024-09_IMG_7761_pBone_plastic_trombone_(crop).jpg)

Trombone "pBone" made from [ABS](/wiki/Acrylonitrile_butadiene_styrene "Acrylonitrile butadiene styrene") plastic

Instruments made mostly from plastic, including the pBone and the Tromba plastic trombone, emerged in the 2010s as a cheaper and more robust alternative to brass.[[29]](#cite_note-pBone-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Plastic instruments could come in almost any colour but the sound plastic instruments produce is different from that of brass. While originally seen as a gimmick, these plastic models have found increasing popularity of the last decade and are now viewed as practice tools that make for more convenient travel as well as a cheaper option for beginning players not wishing to invest so much money in a trombone right away. Manufacturers now produce large-bore models with triggers as well as smaller alto models.

### Regional variations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Regional variations")]

#### Germany and Austria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Germany and Austria")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Kruspe-Tenor-02.02.2023-2.jpg/250px-Kruspe-Tenor-02.02.2023-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kruspe-Tenor-02.02.2023-2.jpg)

Kruspe Virtuosa tenor trombone bell with *Kranz* (wreath)

German trombones have been built in a wide variety of bore and bell sizes. The traditional German *Konzertposaune* can differ substantially from American designs in many aspects. The mouthpiece is typically rather small and is placed into a slide section with a very long leadpipe of at least 12 to 24 inches (30–60 cm). The whole instrument is typically made of gold brass. They are constructed using very thin metal (especially in the bell section), and many have a metal ring called a *Kranz* (lit. 'wreath') on the rim of the bell. Their sound is very even across dynamic levels but it can be difficult to play at louder volumes.[[16]](#cite_note-Friedman-2003-16) While their bore sizes were considered large in the 19th century, German trombones have altered very little over the last 150 years and are now typically somewhat smaller than their American counterparts. Bell sizes remain very large in all sizes of German trombone and a bass trombone bell may exceed 10 inches (25 cm) in diameter.

Valve attachments in tenor and bass trombones were first seen in the mid 19th century, originally on the tenor B♭ trombone. Before 1850, bass trombone parts were mostly played on a slightly longer F-bass trombone (a fourth lower). The first valve was simply a fourth-valve, or in German "Quart-Ventil", built onto a B♭ tenor trombone, to allow playing in low F. This valve was first built without a return spring, and was only intended to set the instrument in B♭ or F for extended passages.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Since the mid-20th century, modern instruments use a trigger to engage the valve while playing.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Thein-SpainKruspe-Tenor-29.04.2023-9.jpg/250px-Thein-SpainKruspe-Tenor-29.04.2023-9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thein-SpainKruspe-Tenor-29.04.2023-9.jpg)

Tuning slide of a trombone with a traditional *Schlangenverzierungen* snake ornament, by Thein Brass

As with other traditional German and Austrian brass instruments, rotary valves are used to the exclusion of almost all other types of valve, even in valve trombones. Other features often found on German trombones include long [water keys](/wiki/Water_key "Water key") as well as *Schlangenverzierungen* (snake decorations) on the slide and bell U-bows to help protect the tubing from damage.

Since around 1925, when jazz music became popular, Germany has been selling "American trombones" as well. Most trombones made and/or played in Germany today, especially by amateurs, are built in the American fashion, as those are much more widely available, and thus far cheaper. However, some higher-end manufacturers such as Thein make modern iterations of the classic German *Konzertposaune*, as well as American-style trombones with German features like the *Kranz* and snake decorations.

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: France")]

French trombones were built in the very smallest bore sizes up to the end of the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and whilst other sizes were made there, the French usually preferred the tenor trombone to any other size. French music, therefore, usually employed a section of three tenor trombones up to the mid–20th century. Tenor trombones produced in France during the 19th and early 20th centuries featured bore sizes of around 0.450 in (11.4 mm), small bells of not more than 6 in (15 cm) in diameter, as well as a funnel-shaped mouthpiece slightly larger than that of the [cornet](/wiki/Cornet "Cornet") or [horn](/wiki/French_horn "French horn"). French tenor trombones were built in both C and B♭, altos in D♭, sopranos in F, piccolos in B♭, basses in G and E♭, and contrabasses in B♭.

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Types")]

The most frequently encountered types of trombone today are the [tenor](/wiki/Tenor_trombone "Tenor trombone") and [bass](/wiki/Bass_trombone "Bass trombone"), though as with many other instrument families such as the [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet"), the trombone has been built in sizes from [piccolo](/wiki/Piccolo_trombone "Piccolo trombone") to [contrabass](/wiki/Contrabass_trombone "Contrabass trombone"). Although trombones are usually constructed with a slide to change the pitch, [valve trombones](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone") instead use the set of three valves common on other brass instruments.

### Slide trombones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Slide trombones")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Trombone_family_composite_3.jpg/500px-Trombone_family_composite_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trombone_family_composite_3.jpg)

Trombones (*top* to *bottom*): [piccolo](/wiki/Piccolo_trombone "Piccolo trombone") in B♭, [soprano](/wiki/Soprano_trombone "Soprano trombone") in B♭, [alto](/wiki/Alto_trombone "Alto trombone") in E♭, [tenor](/wiki/Tenor_trombone "Tenor trombone") in B♭, [bass](/wiki/Bass_trombone "Bass trombone") in B♭ with F and G♭ valves, [contrabass](/wiki/Contrabass_trombone "Contrabass trombone") in F with D and B♭ valves.

#### Contrabass trombone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Contrabass trombone")]

Main article: [Contrabass trombone](/wiki/Contrabass_trombone "Contrabass trombone")

The contrabass trombone is the lowest trombone, first appearing in BB♭ an octave below the tenor with a double slide. This design was commissioned by [Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner") in the 1870s for his *[Der Ring des Nibelungen](/wiki/Der_Ring_des_Nibelungen "Der Ring des Nibelungen")* opera cycle. Since the late 20th century however, it has largely been supplanted by a less cumbersome single-slide bass-contrabass instrument pitched in 12' F. With two valve attachments to provide the same full range as its predecessor, this design is effectively a modern bass trombone built down a [perfect fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth"). Although the contrabass has only appeared occasionally in orchestral repertoire and is not a permanent member of the modern orchestra, it has enjoyed a revival in the 21st century, particularly in film and video game soundtracks.

#### Bass trombone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Bass trombone")]

Main article: [Bass trombone](/wiki/Bass_trombone "Bass trombone")

Although early instruments were pitched in G, F or E♭ below the tenor trombone, the modern bass trombone is pitched in the same B♭ as the tenor but with a wider bore, a larger bell, and a larger mouthpiece. These features facilitate playing in the lower register of the instrument. Modern bass trombones have valves that allow a fully chromatic range down to the pedal register (B♭1). In Britain, the bass trombone in G was used in orchestras from the mid-19th century and survived into the 1950s, particularly in [British brass bands](/wiki/British_brass_band "British brass band").

#### Tenor trombone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Tenor trombone")]

The tenor trombone has a [fundamental](/wiki/Fundamental_frequency "Fundamental frequency") [note](/wiki/Musical_note "Musical note") of B♭ and is usually treated as a [non-transposing instrument](/wiki/Nontransposing_instrument "Nontransposing instrument") (see below). Tenor trombones with C as their fundamental note were almost equally popular in the mid-19th century in Britain and France. As the trombone in its simplest form has neither crooks, valves nor keys to lower the pitch by a specific interval, trombonists use seven chromatic *slide positions*. Each position progressively increases the length of the air column, thus lowering the pitch.

Extending the slide from one position to the next lowers the pitch by one [semitone](/wiki/Semitone "Semitone"). Thus, each note in the [harmonic series](/wiki/Harmonic_series_(music) "Harmonic series (music)") can be lowered by an [interval](/wiki/Interval_(music) "Interval (music)") of up to a [tritone](/wiki/Tritone "Tritone"). The lowest note of the standard instrument is therefore an E♮ – a tritone below B♭. Most experienced trombonists can play lower "[falset](/wiki/Falset_(music) "Falset (music)")" notes and much lower pedal notes (first partials or fundamentals, which have a peculiar metallic rumbling sound). Slide positions are subject to adjustment, compensating for imperfections in the tuning of different harmonics. The fifth partial is rather flat on most trombones and usually requires a minute shortening of the slide position to compensate; other small adjustments are also normally required throughout the range. Trombonists make frequent use of alternate positions to minimize slide movement in rapid passages; for instance, [B♭3](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation") may be played in first or fifth position. Alternate positions are also needed to allow a player to produce a [glissando](/wiki/Glissando "Glissando") to or from a higher note on the same partial.

While the lowest note of the tenor trombone's range (excluding fundamentals or pedal notes) is E2, the trombone's upper range is theoretically open-ended. The practical top of the range is sometimes considered to be F5, or more conservatively D5. The range of the C tenor trombone is F♯2 to G5.

#### Alto trombone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Alto trombone")]

Main article: [Alto trombone](/wiki/Alto_trombone "Alto trombone")

The [alto trombone](/wiki/Alto_trombone "Alto trombone") is smaller than the tenor trombone and almost always pitched in E♭ a fourth higher than the tenor, although examples pitched in F are occasionally found. Modern instruments are sometimes fitted with a valve to lower the pitch, either by a semitone to D (known as a "trill" valve), or by a fourth into B♭. The alto trombone was commonly used in the 16th to the 18th centuries in [church music](/wiki/Church_music "Church music") to strengthen the alto voice, particularly in the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(music) "Mass (music)"). Early 19th century composers such as [Beethoven](/wiki/Beethoven "Beethoven"), [Brahms](/wiki/Brahms "Brahms"), and [Schumann](/wiki/Schumann "Schumann") began writing for alto trombone in their [symphonies](/wiki/Symphony "Symphony"), but the subsequent use and popularity of tenor trombones in the orchestra largely eclipsed their use until a modern revival that began in the late 20th century.

#### Soprano trombone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Soprano trombone")]

Main article: [Soprano trombone](/wiki/Soprano_trombone "Soprano trombone")

The [soprano trombone](/wiki/Soprano_trombone "Soprano trombone") is usually pitched in B♭ an octave above the tenor, and has seldom been used since its first known appearance in 1677 outside of trombone choirs in [Moravian Church music](/wiki/Moravian_Church_music "Moravian Church music"). Built with mouthpiece, bore and bell dimensions similar to the B♭ [trumpet](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"), it tends to be played by trumpet players. During the 20th century some soprano trombones—dubbed *slide cornets*—were made as novelties or for use by jazz players including [Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong") and [Dizzy Gillespie](/wiki/Dizzy_Gillespie "Dizzy Gillespie"). A small number of contemporary proponents of the instrument include jazz artists [Wycliffe Gordon](/wiki/Wycliffe_Gordon "Wycliffe Gordon") and [Christian Scott](/wiki/Christian_Scott_(musician) "Christian Scott (musician)"), and classical trumpeter Torbjörn Hultmark, who advocates for its use as an instrument for young children to learn music.

#### Sopranino and piccolo trombones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Sopranino and piccolo trombones")]

Main article: [Soprano trombone § Sopranino and piccolo trombones](/wiki/Soprano_trombone#Sopranino_and_piccolo_trombones "Soprano trombone")

The sopranino and piccolo trombones appeared in the 1950s as novelty instruments, and are even smaller and higher than the soprano. They are pitched in high E♭ and B♭ respectively, one octave above the alto and soprano trombones. Owing to being essentially a slide variant of the [piccolo trumpet](/wiki/Piccolo_trumpet "Piccolo trumpet"), they are played primarily by trumpet players.

### Trombones with valves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Trombones with valves")]

#### Valve trombone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Valve trombone")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Yamaha_YSL-354_V_valve_trombone.png/250px-Yamaha_YSL-354_V_valve_trombone.png)](/wiki/File:Yamaha_YSL-354_V_valve_trombone.png)

Valve (tenor) trombone in B♭

Main article: [Valve trombone](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone")

In the 19th century as soon as [brass instrument valves](/wiki/Brass_instrument_valve "Brass instrument valve") were invented, trombones with valves instead of slides were adopted widely in orchestras, and remain popular in some parts of Europe and in [military bands](/wiki/Military_band "Military band").

#### Cimbasso

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Cimbasso")]

Main article: [Cimbasso](/wiki/Cimbasso "Cimbasso")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/WANZ_Instrument_2024-09_IMG_7815_%28white_crop%29.png/250px-WANZ_Instrument_2024-09_IMG_7815_%28white_crop%29.png)](/wiki/File:WANZ_Instrument_2024-09_IMG_7815_(white_crop).png)

A modern cimbasso in F

The cimbasso covers the same range as a [tuba](/wiki/Tuba "Tuba") or a [contrabass trombone](/wiki/Contrabass_trombone "Contrabass trombone"). The term *cimbasso* first appeared in early 19th century [Italian opera](/wiki/Italian_opera "Italian opera") scores, and originally referred to an [upright serpent](/wiki/Upright_serpent "Upright serpent") or an [ophicleide](/wiki/Ophicleide "Ophicleide"). The modern cimbasso first appeared as the *trombone basso Verdi* in the 1880s and has three to six [piston](/wiki/Piston_valve "Piston valve") or [rotary valves](/wiki/Rotary_valve "Rotary valve") and a predominantly cylindrical [bore](/wiki/Bore_(wind_instruments) "Bore (wind instruments)"). They are most often pitched in 12' F, although models are available in E♭ and occasionally 16' C and 18' B♭. The cimbasso is most commonly used in performances of late [Romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") Italian operas by [Verdi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Verdi "Giuseppe Verdi") and [Puccini](/wiki/Giacomo_Puccini "Giacomo Puccini"), but has also experienced a 21st-century revival in film, television and video game soundtracks.

#### Superbone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Superbone")]

Main article: [Superbone](/wiki/Superbone "Superbone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/MIMEd_3596._Holton_TR-395_Superbone_%28white%29.png/250px-MIMEd_3596._Holton_TR-395_Superbone_%28white%29.png)](/wiki/File:MIMEd_3596._Holton_TR-395_Superbone_(white).png)

Holton TR-395 Superbone

A hybrid, "duplex" or "double" trombone is a design of trombone that has both a slide and a set of three valves for altering the pitch. It has been reinvented several times since first appearing in the 19th century by [Besson](/wiki/Gustave_Auguste_Besson "Gustave Auguste Besson"), and later [Conn](/wiki/Charles_G._Conn "Charles G. Conn"). Jazz trombonist and machinist [Brad Gowans](/wiki/Brad_Gowans "Brad Gowans") invented his "[valide trombone](/wiki/Valide_trombone "Valide trombone")" in the 1940s with a short four-position slide. In the 1970s [Maynard Ferguson](/wiki/Maynard_Ferguson "Maynard Ferguson") and [Holton](/wiki/Holton_(Leblanc) "Holton (Leblanc)") produced the "Superbone", very similar to the earlier Conn. In 2013 Schagerl in collaboration with [James Morrison](/wiki/James_Morrison_(jazz_musician) "James Morrison (jazz musician)") announced a larger bore variant with rotary valves.

#### Flugabone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Flugabone")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/F.E._Olds_Flugabone.jpg/250px-F.E._Olds_Flugabone.jpg)](/wiki/File:F.E._Olds_Flugabone.jpg)

Flugabone in B♭ by Olds

The "flugabone" (or sometimes "flugelbone"), portmanteau of "[flugelhorn](/wiki/Flugelhorn "Flugelhorn")" and "trombone", also known as the "marching trombone", is a [marching brass](/wiki/Marching_brass "Marching brass") instrument, essentially a [valve trombone](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone") wrapped into a compact flugelhorn shape.[[32]](#cite_note-flugabone-mfrs-32) It retains the [cylindrical bore](/wiki/Bore_(wind_instruments) "Bore (wind instruments)") of the trombone, rather than the conical bore of either the [flugelhorn](/wiki/Flugelhorn "Flugelhorn") or [bugle](/wiki/Bugle "Bugle"), and thus is similar in playing characteristics to a [valve trombone](/wiki/Valve_trombone "Valve trombone"). A similar marching trombone is the "[trombonium](/wiki/Trombonium "Trombonium")" first produced by [King Musical Instruments](/wiki/King_Musical_Instruments "King Musical Instruments"), wrapped and held vertically like a [euphonium](/wiki/Euphonium "Euphonium").

### Other variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Other variants")]

#### Sackbut

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Sackbut")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Sacabutxs%2C_conjunt_al_Museu_de_la_M%C3%BAsica.jpg/250px-Sacabutxs%2C_conjunt_al_Museu_de_la_M%C3%BAsica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sacabutxs,_conjunt_al_Museu_de_la_M%C3%BAsica.jpg)

Alto, tenor and bass sackbuts, [Museu de la Música de Barcelona](/wiki/Museu_de_la_M%C3%BAsica_de_Barcelona "Museu de la Música de Barcelona")

Main article: [Sackbut](/wiki/Sackbut "Sackbut")

The term "sackbut" refers to the early forms of the trombone commonly used during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music") and [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") eras, with a characteristically smaller, more cylindrically proportioned [bore](/wiki/Bore_(wind_instruments) "Bore (wind instruments)"), and a less-flared [bell](/wiki/Bell_(wind_instrument) "Bell (wind instrument)").

#### Buccin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Buccin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Detall_de_bucc%C3%A9n.jpg/250px-Detall_de_bucc%C3%A9n.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detall_de_bucc%C3%A9n.jpg)

Bell of a buccin, Museu de la Música de Barcelona

Main article: [Buccin](/wiki/Buccin "Buccin")

A distinctive form of tenor trombone was popularized in France in the early 19th century. Called the [buccin](/wiki/Buccin "Buccin"), it featured a tenor trombone slide and a bell that ended in a zoomorphic (serpent or dragon) head. It sounds like a cross between a trombone and a [French horn](/wiki/French_horn "French horn"), with a very wide dynamic range but a limited and variable range of pitch. [Hector Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz") wrote for the buccin in his *[Messe solennelle](/wiki/Messe_solennelle_(Berlioz) "Messe solennelle (Berlioz)")* of 1824.

#### Tromboon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Tromboon")]

A [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "trombone" and "bassoon", the "tromboon" was created by musical parodist [Peter Schickele](/wiki/Peter_Schickele "Peter Schickele") by replacing a trombone's [mouthpiece](/wiki/Mouthpiece_(brass) "Mouthpiece (brass)") with the [reed](/wiki/Double_reed "Double reed") and [bocal](/wiki/Bocal "Bocal") of a [bassoon](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon"). It appears in several humorous works of Schickele's fictional composer, [P. D. Q. Bach](/wiki/P._D._Q._Bach "P. D. Q. Bach").

## Technique

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Technique")]

### Basic slide positions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Basic slide positions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Wirth_trombone_slide_position_chart.jpg/250px-Wirth_trombone_slide_position_chart.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wirth_trombone_slide_position_chart.jpg)

Slide position chart (new system); most trombones are tenor trombones, like the valveless one in the middle.

The modern system has seven chromatic [slide positions](/wiki/Position_(music)#Trombone_position "Position (music)") on a tenor trombone in B♭. It was first described by Andre Braun circa 1795.[[33]](#cite_note-Weiner-33)

In 1811 Joseph Fröhlich wrote on the differences between the modern system and an old system where four diatonic slide positions were used and the trombone was usually keyed to A.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuion198893-34) To compare between the two styles the chart below may be helpful (take note for example, in the old system contemporary 1st-position was considered "drawn past" then current 1st).[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuion198893-34) In the modern system, each successive position outward (approximately 3+1⁄4 inches [8 cm]) will produce a note which is one [semitone](/wiki/Semitone "Semitone") lower when played in the same [partial](/wiki/Harmonic_series_(music)#partial "Harmonic series (music)"). Tightening and loosening the lips will allow the player to "bend" the note up or down by a semitone without changing position, so a slightly out-of-position slide may be compensated for by ear.

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| New system | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Old system | – | 1 | – | 2 | – | 3 | 4 |

### Partials and intonation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Partials and intonation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Trombone_first_position_harmonic_series.png/250px-Trombone_first_position_harmonic_series.png)](/wiki/File:Trombone_first_position_harmonic_series.png)

Trombone first position harmonic series, "where additional overtones may be used to stretch the upper range a bit higher."[[19]](#cite_note-Orchestration-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Trombone_seventh_position_harmonic_series.png/250px-Trombone_seventh_position_harmonic_series.png)](/wiki/File:Trombone_seventh_position_harmonic_series.png)

Trombone seventh position harmonic series.[[19]](#cite_note-Orchestration-19)

As with all brass instruments, progressive tightening of the lips and increased air pressure allow the player to move to different [partial](/wiki/Harmonic_series_(music)#partial "Harmonic series (music)") in the [harmonic series](/wiki/Harmonic_series_(music) "Harmonic series (music)"). In the first position (also called closed position) on a B♭ trombone, the notes in the harmonic series begin with B♭2 (one octave higher than the pedal B♭1), F3 (a [perfect fifth](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth") higher than the previous partial), B♭3 (a [perfect fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth") higher), D4 (a [major third](/wiki/Major_third "Major third") higher), and F4 (a [minor third](/wiki/Minor_third "Minor third") higher).

F4 marks the sixth partial, or the fifth overtone. Notes on the next partial, for example A♭4 (a minor third higher) in first position, tend to be out of tune in regards to the twelve-tone [equal temperament](/wiki/Equal_temperament "Equal temperament") scale. A♭4 in particular, which is at the seventh partial (sixth overtone) is nearly always 31 cents, or about one third of a semitone, flat of the minor seventh. On the slide trombone, such deviations from [intonation](/wiki/Intonation_(music) "Intonation (music)") are corrected for by slightly adjusting the slide or by using an alternate position.[[19]](#cite_note-Orchestration-19) Although much of Western music has adopted the even-tempered scale, it has been the practice in Germany and Austria to play these notes in position, where they will have [just intonation](/wiki/Just_intonation "Just intonation") (see [harmonic seventh](/wiki/Harmonic_seventh "Harmonic seventh") as well for A♭4).

The next higher partials—B♭4 (a [major second](/wiki/Major_second "Major second") higher), C5 (a major second higher), D5 (a major second higher)—do not require much adjustment for even-tempered intonation, but E♭5 (a [minor second](/wiki/Minor_second "Minor second") higher) is almost exactly a [quarter tone](/wiki/Quarter_tone "Quarter tone") higher than it would be in twelve-tone equal temperament. E♭5 and F5 (a major second higher) at the next partial are very high notes; a very skilled player with a highly developed [facial musculature](/wiki/Embouchure "Embouchure") and [diaphragm](/wiki/Thoracic_diaphragm "Thoracic diaphragm") can go even higher to G5, A♭5, B♭5 and beyond.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Trombone_F_slide_position_pedal_tones.png/250px-Trombone_F_slide_position_pedal_tones.png)](/wiki/File:Trombone_F_slide_position_pedal_tones.png)

Trombone with F attachment slide position second harmonics.[[19]](#cite_note-Orchestration-19)

The higher in the harmonic series any two successive notes are, the closer they tend to be (as evidenced by the progressively smaller intervals noted above). A byproduct of this is the relatively few motions needed to move between notes in the higher ranges of the trombone. In the lower range, significant movement of the slide is required between positions, which becomes more exaggerated on lower pitched trombones, but for higher notes the player need only use the first four positions of the slide since the partials are closer together, allowing higher notes in alternate positions. As an example, F4 (at the bottom of the treble clef) may be played in first, fourth or sixth position on a B♭ trombone. The note E1 (or the lowest E on a standard 88-key piano keyboard) is the lowest attainable note on a 9-foot (2.7 m) B♭ tenor trombone, requiring a full 7 feet 4 inches (2.24 m) of tubing. On trombones without an F attachment, there is a gap between B♭1 (the fundamental in first position) and E2 (the first harmonic in seventh position). Skilled players can produce "[falset](/wiki/Falset_(music) "Falset (music)")" notes between these, but the sound is relatively weak and not usually used in performance. The addition of an F attachment allows for intermediate notes to be played with more clarity.

### Pedal tones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Pedal tones")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Trombone_slide_position_pedal_tones.png/250px-Trombone_slide_position_pedal_tones.png)](/wiki/File:Trombone_slide_position_pedal_tones.png)

Trombone slide position "pedal tones".[[19]](#cite_note-Orchestration-19)

The B♭ [pedal tone](/wiki/Pedal_tone "Pedal tone") is frequently seen in commercial scoring but much less often in symphonic music, while notes below that are called for only rarely as they "become increasingly difficult to produce and insecure in quality" with A♭ or G being the bottom limit for most tenor trombonists.[[19]](#cite_note-Orchestration-19) The trombone's tubing is largely cylindrical, which inhibits the production of the fundamental as a pedal tone pitch. Instead, trombonists use the higher harmonics of the instrument to produce pedal tones, giving them a bright and hollow tone quality.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Some contemporary orchestral writing, movie or video game scoring, trombone ensemble and solo works will call for notes as low as a pedal C, B, or even double pedal B♭ on the bass trombone.

### Glissando

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Glissando")]

The trombone is one of the few wind instruments that can produce a true [glissando](/wiki/Glissando "Glissando"), by moving the slide without interrupting the airflow or sound production. Every pitch in a glissando must have the same harmonic number, and a tritone is the largest interval that can be performed as a glissando.[[19]](#cite_note-Orchestration-19): 151

The trombone glissando can create remarkable effects, and it is used in jazz and popular music, as in the famous song "[The Stripper](/wiki/The_Stripper "The Stripper")" by David Rose and his orchestra.

'Harmonic', 'inverted', 'broken' or 'false' glissandos are those that cross one or more harmonic series, requiring a simulated or faked glissando effect.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerbert200640-36)

### Trills

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Trills")]

[Trills](/wiki/Trill_(music) "Trill (music)"), though generally simple with valves, are difficult on the slide trombone. Trills tend to be easiest and most effective higher in the harmonic series because the distance between notes is much smaller and slide movement is minimal. For example, a trill on B♭3/C4 is virtually impossible as the slide must move two positions (either 1st-to-3rd or 5th-to-3rd), however at an octave higher (B♭4/C5) the notes can both be achieved in 1st position as a lip trill. Thus, the most convincing trills tend to be above the first octave and a half of the tenor's [range](/wiki/Range_(music) "Range (music)").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerbert200643-37) Trills are most commonly found in early Baroque and Classical music for the trombone as a means of ornamentation, however, some more modern pieces will call for trills as well.

### Notation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Notation")]

Unlike most other brass instruments in an orchestral setting, the trombone is not usually considered a [transposing instrument](/wiki/Transposing_instrument "Transposing instrument"). Prior to the invention of valve systems, most brass instruments were limited to playing one overtone series at a time; altering the pitch of the instrument required manually replacing a section of tubing (called a "[crook](/wiki/Crook_(music) "Crook (music)")") or picking up an instrument of different length. Their parts were transposed according to which crook or length-of-instrument they used at any given time, so that a particular note on the staff always corresponded to a particular partial on the instrument. Trombones, on the other hand, have used slides since their inception. As such, they have always been fully chromatic, so no such tradition took hold, and trombone parts have always been notated at concert pitch (with one exception, discussed below). Also, it was quite common for trombones to double choir parts; reading in concert pitch meant there was no need for dedicated trombone parts. Note that while the fundamental sounding pitch (slide fully retracted) has remained quite consistent, the conceptual pitch of trombones has changed since their origin (*e.g.* Baroque A tenor = modern B-flat tenor).[[38]](#cite_note-Palm-38)

Trombone parts are typically notated in [bass clef](/wiki/Bass_clef "Bass clef"), though sometimes also written in [tenor clef](/wiki/Tenor_clef "Tenor clef") or [alto clef](/wiki/Alto_clef "Alto clef"). The use of alto clef is usually confined to orchestral first trombone parts, with the second trombone part written in tenor clef and the third (bass) part in bass clef. As the alto trombone declined in popularity during the 19th century, this practice was gradually abandoned and first trombone parts came to be notated in the tenor or bass clef. Some Russian and Eastern European composers wrote first and second tenor trombone parts on one alto clef staff (the German [Robert Schumann](/wiki/Robert_Schumann "Robert Schumann") was the first to do this). Examples of this practice are evident in scores by Igor Stravinsky, Sergei Prokofiev, Dmitri Shostakovich. Trombone parts in band music are nearly exclusively notated in bass clef. The rare exceptions are in contemporary works intended for high-level wind bands.

An accomplished performer today is expected to be proficient in reading parts notated in bass clef, tenor clef, alto clef, and (more rarely) treble clef in C, with the British brass-band performer expected to handle treble clef in B♭ as well.

### Mutes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Mutes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Trombone_Plunger.jpg/250px-Trombone_Plunger.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trombone_Plunger.jpg)

A plunger in use

A variety of [mutes](/wiki/Mute_(music) "Mute (music)") can be used with the trombone to alter its [timbre](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre"). Many are held in place with the use of cork grips, including the straight, cup, harmon and pixie mutes. Some fit over the bell, like the bucket mute. In addition to this, mutes can be held in front of the bell and moved to cover more or less area for a [wah-wah](/wiki/Wah-wah_(music) "Wah-wah (music)") effect. Mutes used in this way include the "hat" (a metal mute shaped like a bowler hat) and plunger (which looks like, and often is, the rubber suction cup from a sink or [toilet plunger](/wiki/Plunger "Plunger")). The "wah-wah" sound of a trombone with a harmon mute is featured as the voices of adults in the *[Peanuts](/wiki/Peanuts "Peanuts")* cartoons.

## Didactics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Didactics")]

Several makers have begun to market compact B♭/C trombones that are especially well suited for young children learning to play the trombone who cannot reach the outer slide positions of full-length instruments. The fundamental note of the unenhanced length is C, but the short attachment that puts the instrument in B♭ has its valve *open* when the trigger is *not* depressed. While such instruments have no seventh slide position, C and B natural may be comfortably accessed on the first and second positions by using the trigger. A similar design ("Preacher model") was marketed by [C.G. Conn](/wiki/C.G._Conn "C.G. Conn") in the 1920s, also under the [Wurlitzer](/wiki/Wurlitzer "Wurlitzer") label. Currently, B♭/C trombones are available from many manufacturers, including German makers Günter Frost, Thein and Helmut Voigt, as well as the [Yamaha Corporation](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation").[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Manufacturers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trombone&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Manufacturers")]

Trombones in slide and valve configuration have been made by a vast array of musical instrument manufacturers. For the brass bands of the late 19th and early 20th century, prominent American manufacturers included Graves and Sons, E. G. Wright and Company, [Boston Musical Instrument Company](/wiki/Boston_Musical_Instrument_Company "Boston Musical Instrument Company"), [E. A. Couturier](/wiki/E._A._Couturier "E. A. Couturier"), [H. N. White Company/King Musical Instruments](/wiki/King_Musical_Instruments "King Musical Instruments"), [J. W. York](/wiki/James_Warren_York "James Warren York"), and [C.G. Conn](/wiki/C.G._Conn "C.G. Conn"). In the 21st century, leading mainstream manufacturers of trombones include [Bach](/wiki/Vincent_Bach_Corporation "Vincent Bach Corporation"), [Conn](/wiki/C.G._Conn "C.G. Conn"), Courtois, Edwards, [Getzen](/wiki/Getzen "Getzen"), [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter_Band_Instruments "Jupiter Band Instruments"), King, [Rath](/wiki/Michael_Rath_Trombones "Michael Rath Trombones"), Schilke, S.E. Shires, Thein, Wessex, Willson and [Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation").
